title: The short-term impacts of COVID-19 lockdown on urban air pollution in China
data_sources: China National Urban Air Quality Real-Time Publishing Platform (Ministry of Ecology and Environment)
Wikipedia COVID-19 pandemic lockdown in Hubei + news media + government announcements
2018 China City Statistical Yearbook (National Bureau of Statistics of China)

data_scope.socioeconomic_covariates: GDP, population, industrial output, traffic volume, pollutant emissions (2017/2018)

core_conclusion: COVID-19 city lockdowns in China reduced the AQI by 19.84 points (17%) and PM2.5 by 14.07 µg/m3 (17%) in locked-down cities relative to controls; non-locked-down cities also improved by 6.34 AQI points versus the prior year due to national containment measures. Despite this, PM2.5 remained four times above WHO limits throughout, exposing coal-fired heating as a persistent source and making city lockdowns an unsustainable environmental policy instrument compared with targeted regulation.
why_it_matters: First rigorous causal estimate of lockdown-driven air quality change across all 324 Chinese cities; reveals the floor effect of coal-fired heating even under near-total shutdown; benchmarks lockdown efficiency against conventional environmental policies; documents city-type heterogeneity linking urban industrial and climatic structure to pollution response.
why_nature: Exploits an unprecedented natural experiment at national scale with a clean causal design; directly addresses a core sustainability trade-off (emission reduction versus economic cost); findings are globally transferable; open replication data and code meet Nature Sustainability's reproducibility standards.
added_value_to_my_research: Mobile-phone trajectory + outdoor AQI DiD -> exposure-weighted personal pollution metric for urban health geography
Explainable ML (SHAP) over city covariates -> data-driven city-type archetypes replacing binary heterogeneity splits, bridging to spatially-varying MGWR
Station-level DiD residuals mapped to urban form -> 2SFCA/R2SFCA applied to clean-air access as distributional environmental equity metric
Multi-source data fusion (satellite AOD + ground stations) -> daily gapless PM2.5 surface enabling spatiotemporal KDE of population exposure burden
Lockdown coding as IV -> two-stage design linking AQI changes to social-media-derived mental health signals, connecting environmental economics to health geography

related.title: Increased air pollution exposure among the Chinese population during the national quarantine in 2020
related.relation: companion study revealing that outdoor AQI improvements may be offset by increased indoor exposure during lockdown

related.title: Health co-benefits of post-COVID-19 low-carbon recovery in Chinese cities
related.relation: directly extends this paper's lockdown-emission findings to evaluate green recovery policies in Chinese cities

related.title: Big mobility data reveals hyperlocal air pollution exposure disparities in the Bay Area
related.relation: applies mobility data to reveal intra-city spatial inequality in pollution exposure — extends this paper's city-level analysis to sub-city heterogeneity

related.title: First close insight into global daily gapless 1 km PM2.5 pollution, variability, and health impact
related.relation: provides the ML-based gapless PM2.5 surface methodology needed to extend this paper beyond sparse monitoring stations

related.title: Declines in mental health associated with air pollution and temperature variability in China
related.relation: establishes the pollution-mental health link in China using a similar city-level panel design — natural outcome extension for this paper's lockdown instrument

related.title: Empirical evidence of air pollution reduction from electric vehicle usage across Chinese cities
related.relation: uses a similar city-level empirical design to quantify an alternative emission-reduction mechanism, directly comparable to lockdown effects

figures.caption: Map of locked-down cities — the 95 treatment cities and 229 control cities across China's prefectures
Timing of lockdowns — daily count of locked-down cities from 23 January to 1 March with Spring Festival holiday shading
Event study of lockdown effects — pre-trend validation and post-lockdown AQI divergence with 95% confidence intervals
Heterogeneous lockdown effects — AQI reduction coefficients by climate, wealth, industry, and emission subgroups
